abstract: Transition metal oxides typically undergo dynamic surface reconstruction under oxygen evolution reaction (OER) conditions to achieve their active states, a process that varies significantly according to the electrolyte pH. While this pH-dependent catalytic activity is a widely observed phenomenon, the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this study, we utilize operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy to monitor the evolution of cobalt (Co) oxidation states across various pH-directed (hydr)oxide/electrolyte interfaces. By integrating these observations with in situ electrochemical analysis, we establish critical correlations between Co redox dynamics, flat band potentials, and oxidation state transitions to elucidate the pH dependency of OER activity. Our results demonstrate that alkaline environments lower the flat band potential, facilitating Co redox transformations at lower potentials and promoting beneficial surface reconstruction. Conversely, neutral and acidic environments trigger an anodic shift in these transformations, thereby increasing the catalytic overpotential. In neutral media, this higher overpotential is further driven by limited Co atom polarizability and sluggish oxidation state transitions. These findings underscore that the kinetics and thermodynamics of interfacial Co oxidation state changes are the primary determinants of pH-dependent oxygen evolution efficiency.
